stem: You are the GP reviewing a 34-year-old former paramedic with 8 months of intrusive memories of a multi-fatality road traffic crash, nightmares 3 to 4 nights per week, avoidance of driving on highways, hyperarousal, sleep onset insomnia and irritability. He drinks 6 standard drinks 3 nights per week to sleep. He scores 56 on the PCL-5. He has no current suicidal ideation and has not commenced any treatment. There is no active psychosis. He is willing to engage in therapy or medication.

data_table.Investigations: PCL-5 score 56; LFTs mildly raised (GGT 92); FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Recommend cannabidiol oil under specialist prescribing for sleep and intrusive symptoms; defer formal trauma-focused therapy referral
options.rationale: Cannabidiol is not established first-line for PTSD; trauma-focused CBT or EMDR with concurrent alcohol use management is first-line. (Source: Phoenix Australian Guidelines for PTSD)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily titrated to 200 mg over 8 weeks as first-line monotherapy; defer psychological therapy until response
options.rationale: SSRIs are recommended but psychological therapy is the preferred first-line; pharmacotherapy is offered when therapy is unavailable or insufficient, not as a substitute. (Source: Phoenix Australian Guidelines for PTSD)

options.text: Prescribe oral benzodiazepine (diazepam 5 mg three times daily) for hyperarousal and insomnia; consider therapy at 3-month review
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not recommended for PTSD; they impair fear extinction learning, are addictive and risk worsening avoidance. (Source: Phoenix Australian Guidelines for PTSD)

options.text: Commence quetiapine 25 mg nocte for sleep and arousal; review at 4 weeks to consider trauma-focused therapy referral and engagement
options.rationale: Atypical antipsychotics are not first-line and risk metabolic harm; trauma-focused psychological therapy is the cornerstone of PTSD treatment. (Source: Phoenix Australian Guidelines for PTSD)

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy or EMDR; address harmful alcohol use in parallel with support
options.rationale: Australian PTSD guidance places trauma-focused CBT (or EMDR) as first-line, with concurrent management of comorbid alcohol use as adjunct; SSRI is added if therapy alone is insufficient or unavailable. (Source: Phoenix Australian Guidelines for PTSD)

explanation.summary: Australian PTSD guidance recommends trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy or eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing as first-line treatment, with 8 to 12 sessions. Pharmacotherapy (SSRI: sertraline, paroxetine or venlafaxine) is reserved for patients declining or unable to access therapy, or as adjunct when therapy is insufficient. Benzodiazepines are contraindicated. Antipsychotics are not first-line. Comorbid substance use (especially alcohol) requires concurrent management; complete sobriety is not required to commence therapy. Co-occurring depression, dissociation and chronic pain are common and addressed simultaneously.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT or EMDR is first-line
SSRI as adjunct or when therapy unavailable
Benzodiazepines contraindicated (impair extinction)
Concurrent management of comorbid alcohol use
explanation.pearls: Prazosin 1 to 10 mg nocte has evidence for reducing trauma nightmares and is included as second-line adjunct in Australian guidance for nightmare-predominant PTSD.

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 26-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder diagnosed 18 months ago. She has chronic suicidal ideation, intermittent self-harm by superficial cutting, intense unstable relationships and emotional dysregulation. She is not currently suicidal at this consultation but has cut twice in the last week to manage emotional distress. She has been on sertraline 200 mg for 12 months with no clear benefit. She is engaged with her GP and is willing to try further interventions. There is no acute psychosis.

data_table.Examination: Settled today; superficial linear cuts on left forearm at varied stages of healing; no infection; HEADSS reveals no acute risk
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 score 18, K10 score 32; FBC, TFT, UEC, LFT normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Switch sertraline to fluoxetine and re-titrate over 8 weeks; defer psychological therapy until antidepressant response is established
options.rationale: Switching antidepressants without first addressing underlying personality structure is unlikely to help; structured psychotherapy is the cornerstone. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Add lamotrigine 200 mg daily as a mood stabiliser to address affective instability and recurrent superficial self-harm episodes
options.rationale: Lamotrigine and other mood stabilisers do not show consistent benefit in BPD core symptoms; structured psychotherapy is the recommended first-line. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Admit to inpatient psychiatric unit for prolonged stabilisation and observation; defer outpatient therapy until safety is fully established
options.rationale: Prolonged inpatient admission can worsen BPD by regression, dependence and reinforcement of crisis behaviour; short focused admissions only when immediate safety requires. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Refer for structured psychotherapy (dialectical behaviour therapy or mentalisation-based therapy); review medication once therapy is established
options.rationale: Structured psychotherapy (DBT or MBT) is first-line evidence-based treatment for BPD; pharmacotherapy is adjunctive at best and is reviewed once therapy is in place. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg daily for emotional dysregulation; review weight and full metabolic parameters every 3 months on therapy
options.rationale: Antipsychotics in BPD are used cautiously and short-term for transient psychotic features or severe affective storms; not first-line for chronic dysregulation. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is managed by structured evidence-based psychotherapy: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT), mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema-focused therapy or transference-focused psychotherapy. Australian NHMRC guidance places these as first-line. Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive only: short-term symptom-targeted use of antipsychotics for psychotic features, mood stabilisers for affective storms, antidepressants for comorbid depression. Avoid polypharmacy. Prolonged inpatient admission can worsen outcomes; brief focused admissions only when immediate safety requires. Co-design care plans and crisis plans with the patient.
key_points: DBT or MBT is first-line evidence-based treatment
Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive only; avoid polypharmacy
Brief targeted admissions, not prolonged stays
Co-designed crisis and care plans
explanation.pearls: Validation of distress without endorsing self-harm and clear consistent limit-setting reduces splitting and crisis escalation in primary care.

stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 19-year-old woman with severe anorexia nervosa for medical stabilisation. She has lost 18 kg over 8 months. BMI is 13.4. She has minimal oral intake for 3 weeks. The eating disorders team plans to commence nutritional rehabilitation. Her admission bloods are back. Her ECG shows sinus bradycardia with QTc 478 ms. She has no purging behaviour. She is on no medications. She accepts admission and refeeding under supervision.

data_table.Examination: Cachectic; lanugo; cold peripheries; capillary refill 3 s; muscle wasting; alert oriented; no proximal myopathy yet
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 3.0, phosphate 0.62, magnesium 0.65, sodium 134, glucose 3.6, albumin 32, LFT mildly raised, FBC: Hb 102 microcytic; ECG sinus bradycardia, QTc 478, no U waves

lead_in: What is the most appropriate refeeding plan?

options.text: Commence enteral feeds at 1500 to 2000 kcal/day from day 1 to rapidly correct undernutrition; defer electrolyte correction until refeeding established
options.rationale: High-calorie initiation in severely underweight patients precipitates refeeding syndrome; start at 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day and correct electrolytes first. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Refuse admission for refeeding because BMI 13 is unsafe outside intensive care; arrange community day-programme review next week
options.rationale: BMI under 14 with bradycardia and hypotension requires inpatient medical stabilisation; community care is unsafe at this severity. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold feeds entirely for the first 48 hours and rehydrate with 4 L per day of dextrose 5 percent in saline to correct dehydration first
options.rationale: Carbohydrate-rich rehydration without thiamine and electrolyte correction precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy and severe refeeding syndrome; controlled feeding with thiamine is preferred. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Replace phosphate, potassium, magnesium and start thiamine; commence feeds at 10 kcal/kg/day on day 1 with daily electrolyte monitoring and slow titration
options.rationale: Severe anorexia with electrolyte derangement and bradycardia mandates pre-emptive electrolyte correction, thiamine before any carbohydrate, and cautious caloric ramp from 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolyte monitoring. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Commence total parenteral nutrition at full caloric requirement immediately and defer oral or enteral nutrition for 7 days
options.rationale: Parenteral nutrition is reserved for failure of enteral access and carries higher refeeding risk if initiated at full caloric load; enteral or oral feeding with cautious ramp is preferred. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome arises when carbohydrate-rich nutrition triggers intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium and magnesium in a chronically starved patient, causing arrhythmia, heart failure, seizure, rhabdomyolysis and death. High-risk features include BMI under 16, weight loss over 15 percent, minimal intake for 10 days, or pre-existing electrolyte abnormality. Management: thiamine 100 to 300 mg daily IV or oral starting before any feeds; correct hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypophosphataemia and hypocalcaemia before and during refeeding; commence feeds at 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolyte monitoring; ramp up over 7 to 10 days. Inpatient medical admission for BMI under 14, bradycardia under 40, hypotension, hypothermia, hypoglycaemia or syncope.
key_points: Thiamine 100 to 300 mg before any feed
Correct phosphate, potassium, magnesium pre-emptively
Start feeds 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day, ramp over 7 to 10 days
BMI under 14 + bradycardia requires inpatient admission
explanation.pearls: Hypophosphataemia below 0.5 mmol/L during refeeding triples mortality risk; replace IV phosphate (e.g. potassium phosphate 20 mmol over 6 hours) before increasing calories further.

subtopic_detail: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction GDMT
stem: You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 64-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 28 percent) in clinic 3 months after admission for decompensation. He is now NYHA II symptoms on usual activity. He is on perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, frusemide 40 mg and spironolactone 25 mg, all up-titrated since admission. His latest bloods and observations are stable. He has no peripheral oedema. He is not in atrial fibrillation. He has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.9 percent) on metformin only. His renal function is stable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 68 (sinus), RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: JVP 3 cm, no peripheral oedema, clear chest, normal heart sounds
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 62, K 4.4, Na 138, NT-proBNP 980; HbA1c 6.9; recent echo LVEF 28%
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, frusemide 40 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, metformin 1 g BD

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase perindopril to 10 mg daily and review response in 4 weeks before any additional therapy is added
options.rationale: Up-titration of ACEi is reasonable but the bigger gain is adding the fourth pillar SGLT2 inhibitor, which reduces hospitalisation and mortality in HFrEF irrespective of diabetes status. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Stop spironolactone given normal potassium and stable renal function; add empagliflozin 10 mg daily as the new GDMT pillar
options.rationale: MRA discontinuation in stable HFrEF removes a mortality-reducing pillar; SGLT2i is added in addition, not in place of, MRA. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily to slow heart rate further; defer other medication changes until heart rate is below 60
options.rationale: Ivabradine is added when sinus rhythm with HR 70 or above persists despite maximum tolerated beta-blocker; HR 68 with first-line GDMT incomplete does not warrant ivabradine yet. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Add digoxin 125 micrograms daily for symptom control and review at 6 weeks; defer SGLT2 inhibitor and sacubitril/valsartan
options.rationale: Digoxin is reserved for symptom control after the four pillars are optimised or when AF coexists; it has no mortality benefit. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Switch perindopril to sacubitril/valsartan (after 36-hour washout) and add dapagliflozin 10 mg daily for the fourth pillar of GDMT
options.rationale: Australian guidance for symptomatic HFrEF on ACEi recommends switching to sacubitril/valsartan (after washout) and adding SGLT2 inhibitor; the four pillars (ARNI/ACEi, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) provide additive prognostic benefit. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines)

explanation.summary: HFrEF guideline-directed medical therapy comprises four pillars with additive prognostic benefit: ACEi/ARB (switch to sacubitril/valsartan when tolerated), beta-blocker (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, nebivolol), MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone) and SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Each is up-titrated to target or maximum tolerated dose. SGLT2 inhibitors benefit HFrEF regardless of diabetes status, with risk reduction of 25 to 30 percent for HF hospitalisation and CV death. Ivabradine is added when sinus rhythm with HR 70 or above persists despite optimal beta-blocker.
key_points: Four pillars: ARNI/ACEi, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i
SGLT2 inhibitor benefit independent of diabetes
ARNI requires 36-hour washout from ACEi
Ivabradine for sinus rate 70+ despite optimal beta-blocker
explanation.pearls: Sacubitril/valsartan reduces NT-proBNP within 4 weeks; transient hypotension is the commonest reason for dose reduction during titration.

stem: You are the medicine registrar in ED at 0200 reviewing a 68-year-old man with urinary symptoms for 3 days, now confused. He has a known history of BPH. His GP started oral trimethoprim yesterday. His son brought him in after he became confused this evening. On arrival his observations met sepsis criteria. Blood cultures and urine have been taken. The first set of bloods is back. Cefriaxone 2 g has just been given. He has now received 30 mL/kg crystalloid over 60 minutes. His repeat lactate is back.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.0, HR 124, RR 28, SpO2 96% on 4 L oxygen, BP 78/42 (now MAP 54 after 2.4 L crystalloid), GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Warm peripheries, dry mucous membranes, suprapubic tenderness, no rash, no meningism
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 mmol/L initial, repeat after fluid 3.8; WCC 22 (neutrophils 18), CRP 280, creatinine 168, urea 22, eGFR 38; urine dip 3+ leucocytes 3+ nitrites; ECG normal sinus
data_table.Medications: IV ceftriaxone 2 g given 25 minutes ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch ceftriaxone to oral ciprofloxacin once cultures return; continue conservative management while awaiting microbiology
options.rationale: De-escalation occurs after clinical improvement and culture results; immediate priority in septic shock is haemodynamic stabilisation, not antibiotic switching. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway

options.text: Continue intravenous crystalloid at 1 L per hour for the next 4 hours; defer vasopressor until 6 L has been delivered
options.rationale: Persistent MAP under 65 after 30 mL/kg crystalloid defines fluid-refractory septic shock; ongoing fluid risks pulmonary oedema without improving perfusion, and vasopressor is the next step. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway

options.text: Commence noradrenaline infusion via central access (or peripheral pending central line) to target MAP 65 or above; activate critical care review
options.rationale: Septic shock unresponsive to 30 mL/kg fluid resuscitation requires vasopressor (noradrenaline first-line) to target MAP 65 or above and parallel critical care consultation; further fluid alone risks pulmonary oedema without restoring perfusion. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg as a first-line therapy before considering vasopressor or critical care input
options.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is added in septic shock requiring noradrenaline at moderate dose; it is not first-line ahead of vasopressor. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway

options.text: Insert an indwelling urinary catheter and observe for clinical response over the next 2 hours before any further intervention
options.rationale: Source control is important but septic shock unresponsive to fluid demands immediate vasopressor; catheter alone does not restore perfusion. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway

explanation.summary: Septic shock is defined by sepsis plus persistent hypotension requiring vasopressor to maintain MAP 65 or above AND serum lactate over 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotic within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4, vasopressor (noradrenaline first-line) if MAP under 65 despite fluid. Source control as soon as feasible. Stress-dose hydrocortisone 200 mg per day is added in fluid- and vasopressor-refractory shock. Critical care input is required.
key_points: Septic shock: vasopressor + lactate over 2 despite fluid
Noradrenaline first-line vasopressor
Lactate, cultures, antibiotics within first hour
Hydrocortisone for refractory shock on rising vasopressor
explanation.pearls: Peripheral noradrenaline can be safely commenced through a large bore (16G or larger) antecubital cannula for up to 24 hours while central access is arranged; do not delay vasopressor waiting for central line.

sources.label: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / SA Health Sepsis Pathway

subtopic_detail: DOAC selection in AF with chronic kidney disease
stem: You are the general medicine registrar admitting a 78-year-old woman in newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. She has a CHA2DS2-VA score of 5 (hypertension, age, heart failure, female sex). She has chronic kidney disease stage 3b (eGFR 32 mL/min). She has no mechanical valve, no significant mitral stenosis and no recent bleeding. Her weight is 56 kg. She is on no anticoagulant. Liver function is normal. She has no history of intracranial bleed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82, HR 118 (irregular), RR 18, SpO2 97% RA, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse; clear chest; no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: ECG AF rate 118; eGFR 32; creatinine 145; INR 1.0; LFT normal; Hb 124, platelets 220; TFT normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation choice?

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg daily as the preferred once-daily DOAC in CKD stage 3b to improve adherence and minimise bleed risk
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is acceptable in CKD stage 3 at reduced dose but apixaban has lower renal dependence and lower major bleed risk in this population. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Warfarin to a target INR of 2.0 to 3.0 because all DOACs are contraindicated when eGFR is below 50 mL/min
options.rationale: DOACs are permitted across CKD stages 1 to 4 with dose adjustment; apixaban is preferred in CKD stage 3 to 4 because of lowest renal clearance. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Dabigatran 110 mg twice daily as the preferred DOAC in renal impairment because of available reversal with idarucizumab
options.rationale: Dabigatran is 80 percent renally cleared and is contraindicated when eGFR is under 30 and used cautiously between 30 and 50; apixaban remains preferred in CKD. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily (or 2.5 mg twice daily if 2 of 3 dose-reduction criteria are met) as the preferred DOAC in CKD stage 3b
options.rationale: Apixaban has the lowest renal clearance of the DOACs and is preferred in CKD stage 3b; dose reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily only when 2 of 3 criteria met (age 80+, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 micromol/L or above). (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as monotherapy because anticoagulation carries unacceptable risk in CKD stage 3b regardless of CHA2DS2-VA score
options.rationale: Aspirin monotherapy is not effective stroke prevention in AF and is not recommended; anticoagulation is indicated based on CHA2DS2-VA. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

explanation.summary: Anticoagulation in atrial fibrillation is guided by the CHA2DS2-VA score in Australia (replacing CHA2DS2-VASc, sex no longer a modifier). Score 1 or higher: anticoagulation indicated. DOACs are preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF. Choice in CKD: apixaban is preferred in CKD stages 3 to 4 due to lowest renal clearance (27 percent); dabigatran is contraindicated when eGFR is under 30 and used cautiously 30 to 50; rivaroxaban dose-reduced to 15 mg daily for eGFR 15 to 49. Apixaban dose reduces to 2.5 mg twice daily only if 2 of 3 criteria met: age 80 or above, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 or above. Warfarin remains preferred for mechanical valves and significant mitral stenosis.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VA score (sex removed) guides anticoagulation
Apixaban preferred in CKD stage 3 to 4
Dabigatran contraindicated eGFR under 30
Apixaban dose reduction: 2 of 3 of age 80+, weight 60 or less, creatinine 133+
explanation.pearls: Andexanet alfa is the specific reversal agent for apixaban and rivaroxaban; for dabigatran, idarucizumab reverses fully within 5 minutes for emergency procedures or major bleeding.

stem: You are the medicine registrar called to ED to review a 72-year-old man with chronic kidney disease stage 4 who presents with weakness and palpitations. He is on ramipril, spironolactone and recently started trimethoprim for a UTI. He has not been able to take his medications today. He has no chest pain. Bloods returned 5 minutes ago. The ED ECG was taken urgently and is in front of you. He is haemodynamically stable. He is not in cardiac arrest.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/72, HR 58 (regular), RR 18, SpO2 98% RA, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Generalised weakness, no focal neurology, no cardiac murmur, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: K 7.2 mmol/L, urea 28, creatinine 320, eGFR 16, Na 134, HCO3 17, glucose 6.8; ECG: peaked T waves, widened QRS 132 ms, no P waves
data_table.Medications: Ramipril 10 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, trimethoprim 300 mg (started 3 days ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management sequence?

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g and observe over the next 4 hours before any other intervention or further blood test review
options.rationale: Resin therapy has slow onset (hours) and is inadequate as the sole acute intervention; calcium plus insulin/dextrose are required for ECG changes. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50% dextrose alone; no calcium gluconate because the patient is haemodynamically stable currently
options.rationale: QRS widening on ECG indicates membrane instability and calcium gluconate is the immediate priority; insulin acts via slower potassium redistribution. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes for membrane stabilisation, then insulin/dextrose and salbutamol nebuliser
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia with QRS widening is a cardiac emergency; calcium gluconate stabilises the membrane within minutes, followed by insulin/dextrose and salbutamol for intracellular shift, then potassium removal (resin or dialysis) and discontinuation of contributing drugs. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guideline)

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis as the first intervention without calcium gluconate, insulin or salbutamol while waiting for vascular access placement
options.rationale: Dialysis is definitive treatment for hyperkalaemia but takes hours to arrange; immediate calcium and shifters must be given in the interim to protect the myocardium. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guideline)

options.text: Frusemide 80 mg IV bolus to promote kaliuresis as the first-line intervention before considering calcium gluconate or shifting therapy
options.rationale: Loop diuretic effectiveness depends on functional nephrons; in advanced CKD with anuria or oliguria, response is limited and calcium stabilisation is needed first. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia (potassium 6.5 mmol/L or above, or any level with ECG changes) requires staged emergency management. Step 1: cardiac membrane stabilisation with IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes (effect within 3 minutes, lasts 30 to 60 minutes; repeat if ECG changes persist). Step 2: intracellular shift with IV insulin 10 units plus 50 mL 50 percent dextrose (effect 15 to 30 minutes); add nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg as adjunct shifter. Step 3: potassium removal via resin (sodium zirconium cyclosilicate, patiromer) or dialysis. Step 4: stop ACE inhibitor, MRA, trimethoprim and other contributing drugs. Underlying acidosis correction with bicarbonate is reserved for metabolic acidosis with pH below 7.2.
key_points: Calcium gluconate stabilises membrane within 3 minutes
Insulin/dextrose and salbutamol shift potassium intracellularly
Resins and dialysis remove potassium
Stop ACEi, MRA, trimethoprim and other contributors
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim blocks the epithelial sodium channel similarly to amiloride, causing hyperkalaemia especially when combined with ACEi or MRA; check potassium within 7 days of starting if on these medications.

stem: You are the medicine registrar called to ED to review a 56-year-old man with type 2 diabetes who presents with a 36-hour history of escalating left lower limb pain. He noticed redness yesterday after a minor abrasion. The pain has rapidly worsened overnight, out of proportion to the visible erythema. He is now febrile and confused. Examination shows tense oedema, dusky skin discolouration with one violaceous patch, and crepitus on palpation. The team is debating cellulitis versus necrotising fasciitis.

data_table.Examination: Tense oedema entire calf; dusky discolouration with violaceous patch; crepitus on palpation; pain disproportionate to visible erythema; reduced sensation over dusky area
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 (neutrophils 18), CRP 320, sodium 128, glucose 18, creatinine 156, lactate 5.2; venous gas: pH 7.28, lactate 5.2; LRINEC score 9
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD; gliclazide 60 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Urgent surgical consultation for emergency debridement; broad-spectrum IV antibiotics including clindamycin; aggressive resuscitation
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency; immediate debridement, broad antibiotics with anti-toxin coverage (clindamycin) and aggressive resuscitation reduce mortality; LRINEC 8 or above and pain out of proportion are red flags. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence oral cephalexin 500 mg every 6 hours for cellulitis; mark the erythema and review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral cellulitis treatment is inappropriate for systemic toxicity, crepitus, dusky skin and disproportionate pain; necrotising fasciitis demands surgical and IV antibiotic emergency response. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer IV vancomycin alone; defer surgical consultation pending blood culture results to confirm a microbiological diagnosis
options.rationale: Antibiotic alone without surgical debridement does not control necrotising soft tissue infection; surgical exploration is the diagnostic and therapeutic gold standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Order urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the limb and defer empirical antibiotics until imaging confirms fascial involvement
options.rationale: Imaging delays definitive surgical management; clinical suspicion plus LRINEC 8+ mandates immediate surgical consultation regardless of imaging. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Elevate the limb, commence subcutaneous low molecular weight heparin and observe over 6 hours for spread of erythema
options.rationale: Observation in suspected necrotising fasciitis is fatal; hourly progression of fascial necrosis demands immediate surgical exploration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency with mortality of 20 to 40 percent. Clinical clues: pain out of proportion to skin findings, rapid progression, dusky or violaceous skin, crepitus, bullae, anaesthesia over the involved area, systemic toxicity. LRINEC score uses CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine and glucose; score 6 to 7 raises suspicion, 8 or above is high risk. Management: immediate surgical exploration and debridement, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with clindamycin (toxin suppression) plus piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem and vancomycin for MRSA cover, aggressive resuscitation, ICU admission. Imaging should not delay surgery.
key_points: Pain out of proportion is the cardinal early sign
LRINEC score: 6-7 suspicion, 8+ high risk
Clindamycin for toxin suppression in addition to broad cover
Imaging must not delay surgical exploration
explanation.pearls: Type II necrotising fasciitis (group A Streptococcus) classically affects healthy patients after minor trauma; type I is polymicrobial in diabetes and surgical sites and includes Fournier gangrene.

stem: You are the endocrinology registrar reviewing a 48-year-old woman with biochemically confirmed phaeochromocytoma (24-hour fractionated metanephrines 8-fold raised, normetanephrines 6-fold raised). Imaging shows a 5 cm right adrenal mass. Surgery is planned in 3 weeks. She has episodes of paroxysmal hypertension, sweating and palpitations. She is on no medications currently. Her ECG shows sinus rhythm 84. She is asking about preoperative preparation. There is no history of asthma. She has heart failure symptoms only with severe episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 152/94 between paroxysms (peaks to 220/130 during episodes), HR 84, RR 14, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Fine tremor; mild orthostatic drop; no abdominal mass palpable; no thyroid mass
data_table.Investigations: 24h fractionated metanephrines 8x ULN; normetanephrines 6x ULN; chromogranin A raised; CT: 5 cm right adrenal heterogeneous mass; MIBG positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate preoperative management plan?

options.text: Commence atenolol 50 mg daily first for tachycardia control; add phenoxybenzamine in the final 48 hours before surgery only as a brief titration
options.rationale: Starting beta-blocker before alpha blockade removes vasodilatory beta-2 effect and unmasks unopposed alpha vasoconstriction, precipitating severe hypertension and myocardial ischaemia. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Commence oral phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily and titrate over 10 to 14 days; add beta-blocker only after adequate alpha blockade is established
options.rationale: Non-selective alpha blockade (phenoxybenzamine) is started first and titrated over 10 to 14 days for adequate vascular volume expansion and symptom control; beta-blocker is added only after alpha blockade is established to avoid unopposed alpha-mediated hypertensive crisis. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparoscopic adrenalectomy within 7 days without preoperative medical blockade; manage crises with intraoperative nitroprusside
options.rationale: Unblocked surgery causes catastrophic intraoperative haemodynamic instability and mortality; preoperative alpha blockade for 10 to 14 days plus volume expansion is standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Administer captopril 25 mg three times daily as first-line preoperative antihypertensive; reserve alpha blockade for refractory cases only
options.rationale: Catecholamine-driven hypertension responds poorly to ACE inhibitors; alpha blockade with volume expansion is the proven preoperative standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Commence labetalol monotherapy 100 mg twice daily; it provides combined alpha and beta blockade in a single agent without sequencing issues
options.rationale: Labetalol provides only weak alpha blockade relative to beta blockade (alpha to beta ratio approximately 1 to 7 oral), risking the same unopposed alpha effect as pure beta blockers; non-selective alpha blocker first is the standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma preoperative blockade requires sequential alpha then beta blockade over 10 to 14 days. Phenoxybenzamine (non-selective, irreversible alpha blocker) is started at 10 mg twice daily and titrated to symptom control, target BP under 130/80 seated and orthostasis. High sodium diet and volume expansion accompany alpha blockade because chronic catecholamine excess depletes intravascular volume. Beta-blocker (propranolol or atenolol) is added only after alpha blockade is established, to control reflex tachycardia. Starting beta-blocker first removes vasodilatory beta-2 tone and unmasks alpha-mediated vasoconstriction, precipitating hypertensive crisis. Labetalol has insufficient alpha component for monotherapy.
key_points: Alpha blockade first for 10 to 14 days
High sodium diet and volume expansion alongside alpha block
Beta-blocker added only after alpha blockade established
Labetalol alpha to beta ratio insufficient for monotherapy
explanation.pearls: Alternative selective alpha-1 blockers (doxazosin, prazosin) are reversible and shorter acting than phenoxybenzamine; intraoperative haemodynamic stability is slightly worse but postoperative hypotension less prolonged.

stem: You are the medicine registrar reviewing a 68-year-old man brought to ED by ambulance with right-sided weakness and expressive aphasia. His last known well time was 2 hours 30 minutes ago. He is on apixaban 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation, last dose 14 hours ago. He has no recent surgery, no head trauma, no known intracranial pathology and no recent bleed. His NIHSS is 14. Non-contrast CT brain shows no haemorrhage and no large established infarct; CTA shows a left middle cerebral artery M1 occlusion.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 172/96, HR 84 (irregular), RR 16, SpO2 97% RA, T 36.6, BSL 6.2
data_table.Examination: Right hemiplegia, expressive aphasia, right facial droop, NIHSS 14, GCS 14
data_table.Investigations: Non-contrast CT: no haemorrhage, ASPECTS 9; CTA: left M1 occlusion; INR 1.0; FBC, UEC normal; platelets 248
data_table.Medications: Apixaban 5 mg BD (last dose 14 hours ago); perindopril 5 mg; atorvastatin 40 mg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Administer IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg over 1 hour and proceed to endovascular thrombectomy without delay for the proximal large vessel occlusion
options.rationale: Apixaban use within 48 hours is a contraindication to IV thrombolysis regardless of last dose timing; thrombectomy alone is preferred when thrombolysis is contraindicated. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous heparin infusion targeting an APTT of 60 to 80 seconds and monitor for clinical improvement over the next 4 hours observation
options.rationale: Heparin has no role in acute ischaemic stroke reperfusion and increases haemorrhagic transformation risk; mechanical thrombectomy is the indicated reperfusion strategy. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Reverse apixaban with andexanet alfa and then administer IV tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg single bolus; defer thrombectomy until thrombolysis is complete
options.rationale: Routine pre-thrombolysis DOAC reversal with andexanet is not recommended due to rebound thrombosis risk; mechanical thrombectomy is the preferred reperfusion strategy. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer aspirin 300 mg orally and admit to stroke unit; defer mechanical thrombectomy because the time window has been exceeded for reperfusion
options.rationale: Mechanical thrombectomy time window extends to 6 hours from onset and up to 24 hours with perfusion imaging selection; this patient at 2.5 hours qualifies; aspirin alone forfeits proven reperfusion benefit. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold thrombolysis due to DOAC use within 48 hours; proceed directly to endovascular thrombectomy for the proven large vessel occlusion
options.rationale: DOAC within 48 hours is a contraindication to thrombolysis but does not preclude mechanical thrombectomy, which is the standard of care for proximal MCA occlusion within 6 hours (and up to 24 hours with imaging selection). (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute ischaemic stroke reperfusion is governed by time window and contraindications. IV thrombolysis (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg or tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg) is offered within 4.5 hours of onset. DOAC use within 48 hours contraindicates thrombolysis unless DOAC level is undetectable or reversed. Mechanical thrombectomy is offered for proven large vessel occlusion (ICA, M1, basilar) within 6 hours of onset, extending to 24 hours with favourable perfusion imaging (DAWN, DEFUSE-3 criteria). DOAC use does not contraindicate thrombectomy. BP target before thrombolysis is under 185/110 and during the first 24 hours under 180/105.
key_points: DOAC within 48 hours contraindicates thrombolysis
DOAC does NOT contraindicate mechanical thrombectomy
Thrombectomy window 6 hours, up to 24 hours with imaging
BP target under 185/110 pre-thrombolysis
explanation.pearls: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg single bolus is non-inferior to alteplase for stroke thrombolysis with simpler administration; Australian centres are increasingly switching to tenecteplase as first-line.
